name: Bazargan
status_notes: Busiest Iran–Turkey crossing. Open as of Jan 2026.
visa_notes: No VOA at land borders. Must have Iran visa before arrival.
visa_other_side_notes: Turkey: Most nationalities 90-day visa-free. US/CA/AU/UK need e-Visa ($50).
vehicle_notes: Busiest vehicle crossing. Truck queues can be enormous — tourist vehicles usually get priority lane. Carnet stamped on Iranian side.
carnet_notes: Carnet de Passage required for all motor vehicles. Bicycle does NOT need carnet. Since April 2024, vehicles with international plates no longer need temporary Iranian plates.
tips_for_tourists: Most popular entry for Europeans. The Iranian side is efficient for tourists — separate from commercial trucks. Money changers offer fair rates. Buy an Irancell SIM immediately at the shops outside customs. Maku (15km) has basic hotels; Tabriz (300km) is the first major city. Shared taxis to Tabriz available.
tips_for_cyclists: Ride through — no need to load bike onto transport. Iranian customs will inspect panniers. The road from Bazargan to Maku is good quality with a shoulder. Tabriz is 300km — plan 3 days of cycling through dramatic mountain scenery. Water available in villages along the way.
tips_for_motorcyclists: Carnet stamped here. Insurance purchased at border or in Maku. Keep engine capacity documents ready — bikes over 250cc are technically restricted but virtually all tourists pass without issue. If questioned, remain calm and polite. Road to Tabriz is excellent two-lane highway.
tips_for_vehicles: Separate lane for tourist vehicles. Carnet stamped and vehicle details checked against registration. Insurance MUST be purchased if not already covered. Process takes 1-3 hours depending on traffic. Fuel up in Turkey — Iranian fuel is cheap but first station is 15km in Maku.
description: The main gateway between Europe and Iran. Gürbulak/Bazargan is Iran's busiest land border crossing with Turkey, used by the vast majority of overland travelers entering from the west. Located in the mountainous northwest near Mount Ararat, it connects Turkey's eastern Doğubayazıt region with Iran's West Azerbaijan province.

name: Razi
status_notes: Open as of Jan 2026. Rail crossing also operates (Tehran–Ankara train when running).
tips_for_tourists: Quieter alternative to Bazargan. Better if coming from Van or southeastern Turkey. Less truck traffic means faster processing. The train crossing here occasionally operates for the Trans-Asia Express (Istanbul–Tehran) — check current schedules.
tips_for_cyclists: Good quiet alternative. Road from Khoy to Tabriz (180km) passes through scenic countryside. Less traffic than the Bazargan–Tabriz highway.

name: Serow
status_notes: Open as of Jan 2026. Most remote of the three Turkey crossings.
tips_for_tourists: Least used of the three Turkey crossings. Very mountainous and remote on both sides. Only use if your itinerary specifically takes you through Hakkari region. Limited onward transport — may need to arrange private taxi.

name: Norduz
status_notes: Consistently open. Armenia's only border with Iran. Reliable crossing even during 2025 tensions.
visa_notes: No Iran VOA at land borders.
visa_other_side_notes: Armenia: Most nationalities 180-day visa-free with valid passport. Very welcoming.
tips_for_tourists: Iran's easiest and most reliable border crossing. Small, efficient, friendly on both sides. From Iranian side, Jolfa (60km) is a pleasant town with caravanserai accommodation. From Armenian side, Meghri is just 3km — shared taxis to Kapan and onward to Yerevan. Beautiful Aras river valley scenery.
tips_for_cyclists: Excellent cycling border. Low traffic road from Jolfa through the Aras valley. The Armenian side climbs steeply from Meghri to Kapan (70km, significant elevation). Stock up on water — remote section. Iran→Armenia direction is a stunning descent.
tips_for_motorcyclists: Straightforward carnet stamp. Road quality is good on both sides. The Jolfa→Tabriz road (140km) is scenic and well-maintained. On the Armenian side, roads are narrower but paved.
description: Iran's sole border crossing with Armenia. A small, peaceful crossing at the Aras river gorge. Popular with overlanders doing the Caucasus circuit (Georgia → Armenia → Iran → back). Consistently reliable even during regional tensions.

name: Astara
status_notes: Azerbaijan land borders have been closed since 2020 (COVID). Special authorization sometimes granted — requires embassy facilitation. Status as of Feb 2026: closed for general tourist traffic. May reopen — check Azerbaijan MFA for latest.
verified_source: US Virtual Embassy Iran, Feb 2026; TripAdvisor reports
tips_for_tourists: Do NOT plan your trip around this crossing being open unless you have confirmed authorization from Azerbaijan embassy. Many travelers have been turned away. Alternative: fly Baku→Tehran, or route through Georgia→Armenia→Iran.

name: Jolfa
status_notes: Nakhchivan exclave crossing. Same restriction as mainland Azerbaijan. Land borders closed since 2020.
tips_for_tourists: Currently closed. When open, this connects to Azerbaijan's Nakhchivan exclave — not mainland Azerbaijan. Limited onward options from Nakhchivan.

name: Bileh Savar
status_notes: Closed since 2020. Same situation as Astara.

name: Bajgiran
status_notes: Open but Turkmenistan requires special authorization. Very difficult visa. Overlanders report mixed experiences. As of Feb 2026, open with prior embassy approval.
verified_source: US Virtual Embassy, Horizons Unlimited reports
tips_for_tourists: Turkmenistan transit visa (5-day) is the practical option. Full tourist visa requires a guide and is expensive. The crossing itself is straightforward — the visa is the hard part. From Iranian side, Mashhad (200km) is the staging city. Shared taxis from Quchan to the border.
tips_for_motorcyclists: Carnet stamped on both sides. Turkmenistan has strict transit routes — you MUST follow the approved route and exit within your visa days. Fuel is insanely cheap in Turkmenistan (~$0.02/liter).

name: Sarakhs
status_notes: Open with same Turkmenistan visa restrictions. Rail crossing for Tehran–Ashgabat train (when running).
tips_for_tourists: Alternative to Bajgiran. Better for travelers heading to Mary/Merv rather than Ashgabat. Less busy. Check if the train is running — it's a memorable journey.

name: Tamarchin
status_notes: Open. Connects to Iraqi Kurdistan (KRI), not rest of Iraq. One of the more scenic border crossings in the Middle East.
tips_for_tourists: Enters Iraqi Kurdistan — separate visa system from federal Iraq. KRI offers visa-on-arrival for most nationalities. Scenic mountain crossing. Iranian side is efficient. The road to/from Piranshahr passes through beautiful Kurdish landscapes. Not recommended for travel beyond KRI into federal Iraq.

name: Khosravi
status_notes: Open for pilgrimage and commercial traffic. NOT recommended for tourists. Federal Iraq side has security concerns.
tips_for_tourists: Avoid. This border is for pilgrims going to Karbala/Najaf and commercial traffic. Not set up for tourist processing. Security situation on Iraqi side is unpredictable.

name: Mehran
status_notes: Pilgrimage and commercial crossing. Not for tourists.

name: Shalamcheh
status_notes: Pilgrimage crossing to Basra. Not for general tourists.

name: Mirjaveh
status_notes: Open but EXTREME CAUTION advised. Sistan-Baluchestan has security concerns. Many governments advise against all travel in this region. Rail crossing exists (Zahedan–Quetta train, infrequent).
tips_for_tourists: This is the ONLY realistic Iran–Pakistan crossing for overlanders. However, Sistan-Baluchestan province has active security threats on BOTH sides. Most governments advise against travel here. If you must cross: arrange armed escort through Pakistani Baluchistan (mandatory — provided free by Levies Force). Travel in convoy. Do NOT stop unnecessarily. On Iranian side, Zahedan is functional but not a tourist destination. The Zahedan–Quetta train runs sporadically — check schedule.
tips_for_cyclists: Extremely difficult crossing for cyclists. The Pakistan side (Taftan to Quetta, 700km) passes through dangerous territory. Armed escort escorts you in trucks — your bike goes on the truck too. Iran side (Zahedan to Bam/Kerman, 500km) is empty desert with extreme heat. Carry 10+ liters of water minimum.
tips_for_motorcyclists: Same security concerns. On the Pakistan side, you'll likely be required to ride in a convoy with escort. On Iran side, fuel stations are sparse — carry extra. Road quality is decent but monotonous desert.

name: Rimdan
status_notes: Open but NOT for general tourist use. Remote coastal crossing. Very high security risk area.

name: Dogharoun
status_notes: Open for commercial and refugee traffic. ALL major governments advise against travel to Afghanistan. Taliban-controlled territory on the other side.
tips_for_tourists: DO NOT USE for tourism. This is an active commercial/humanitarian corridor, not a tourist crossing.

name: Milak
status_notes: Commercial crossing. Do not use for tourism.

restriction_notes: Bicycles enter freely. No special paperwork beyond your passport and visa. Customs may briefly inspect panniers.
documents_notes: That's it! Bicycles are the simplest vehicle to bring into Iran. No carnet, no insurance, no plates required.
road_quality_notes: Main highways have shoulders suitable for cycling. Secondary roads vary — some excellent, some rough. Desert sections can have sand on road edges. Mountain passes have good surfaces but steep grades. Avoid cycling in major cities (Tehran, Isfahan, Mashhad) — traffic is chaotic.
repair_availability: Basic bicycle repair shops in most towns. High-quality parts (Shimano, etc.) mainly available in Tehran. Carry essential spares: tubes, brake pads, chain links, cables. Iranian mechanics are resourceful and will improvise fixes.
spare_parts_notes: Tehran: Iran Mall area has good bike shops. Isfahan: shops near Naqsh-e Jahan. Shiraz: limited. Carry your own touring-specific parts.
breakdown_assistance: Flag someone down — Iranians will go out of their way to help a stranded cyclist. Many will insist on driving you to the nearest town.
guide_requirement_notes: Since Sep 2025, travelers from EU/US/UK/CA/AU/NZ need licensed guide for entire trip — this applies to cyclists too. The guide doesn't need to ride with you but must be coordinating your route and accommodations.

restriction_notes: Under 250cc motorcycles are fully legal. No issues at any border.
insurance_notes: Third-party insurance can be purchased at the border or in the nearest city. ~$30 for 30 days.
fuel_notes: Gasoline ~$0.08/liter (subsidized). Tourists can buy at pumps without Iranian fuel card. Some stations may charge slightly more (~$0.15). Stations in all cities and every 50-80km on highways.
carnet_issuing_body: TACI - Touring & Automobile Club of Iran
carnet_deposit_notes: Iran classified as AIT/FIA Band 7 (high). Deposit can be significant. Check with your national automobile association (ADAC, RAC/CARS UK, AAA AU, TCS, etc.).
triptych_notes: Triptych booklet available from TACI for single-country entry. Up to 10 entries. Alternative to full CDP.
road_quality_notes: Iranian main roads are well-maintained. Highway surfaces are good. Watch for potholes on secondary roads. Gravel sections in remote areas. Desert sand encroachment on some roads in Sistan-Baluchestan and south Khorasan.
repair_availability: Iranian mechanics are legendary for motorcycle repairs. Small Honda/Yamaha parts widely available (Iran produces Honda CG125 clones). Foreign brand parts harder to find — carry essentials.
guide_requirement_notes: Since Sep 2025: guide requirement applies. Guide coordinates your itinerary but doesn't need to ride pillion.

restriction_notes: TECHNICALLY restricted: Iranian law limits >250cc motorcycles to 5-day stay. IN PRACTICE: ~95% of foreign tourists on big bikes enter without issue and stay the full visa duration. Border officials understand these are touring bikes, not permanent imports. Keep documents ready, be polite, and expect to spend extra time at customs. US-made motorcycles (Harley-Davidson, Indian) are explicitly banned.
banned_notes: US-made motorcycles are prohibited from entry. All other brands (BMW, KTM, Honda, Yamaha, Triumph, Ducati, etc.) are fine in practice.
repair_availability: BMW GS, KTM, Honda Africa Twin parts NOT available locally. Carry: oil filters, brake pads, levers, inner tubes (if applicable), chain and sprockets, spark plugs. Iranian mechanics can fabricate many parts. Tehran has a few shops familiar with large adventure bikes.
guide_requirement_notes: Guide requirement applies per Sep 2025 rules.

restriction_notes: Since April 2024, vehicles with international plates no longer need temporary Iranian plates. Previously a major friction point — now resolved. Luxury vehicles (Porsche, Lamborghini, Ferrari, Maserati, Bugatti) and all US-made cars are banned.
banned_notes: US-made vehicles and luxury/high-end vehicles are prohibited. Since 2019, the $40,000 value cap and 2500cc engine limit have been lifted for most vehicles.
insurance_notes: Mandatory third-party. ~$50 for 30 days. Can purchase at border or first major city. Comprehensive coverage available from Iranian insurers.
road_quality_notes: Iran has excellent highway infrastructure. Multi-lane expressways connect major cities. Tehran–Isfahan, Tehran–Mashhad, Isfahan–Shiraz are all high-quality roads. Mountain passes in Zagros and Alborz are scenic but sometimes steep/winding. Remote desert roads in Sistan-Baluchestan can be rough.
police_checkpoint_notes: Frequent police checkpoints throughout Iran. Always have passport, visa, carnet, registration, and insurance accessible. Officers are generally friendly and curious about foreign vehicles. Some may want a selfie with your car.
repair_availability: Mechanics everywhere. Iran produces Peugeot 206/405 and SAIPA cars domestically — parts for these are everywhere. Parts for other European/Japanese cars available in major cities. Specialist 4×4 shops in Tehran.
guide_requirement_notes: Guide requirement applies per Sep 2025 rules.

restriction_notes: Campervans and motorhomes are welcome. Wild camping is legal and widely practiced. Since 2019, engine size and value restrictions have been lifted. No US-made vehicles.
fuel_notes: Diesel also available at subsidized rates. LPG refills possible in major cities.
road_quality_notes: Most highways accommodate campervans. Some mountain roads have tight switchbacks. Watch height restrictions on older bridges. Tehran traffic is brutal in large vehicles — avoid. Parking in city centers can be challenging.
repair_availability: Sprinter/Mercedes vans well-known in Iran. Parts available. Specialist campervan services in Tehran. For other chassis, carry spares.

name: The Classic Route: Tehran → Kashan → Isfahan → Yazd → Shiraz
short_description: Iran's most iconic overland route through the cultural heartland

waypoints.name: Tehran
waypoints.notes: Start point. Visit Golestan Palace, Grand Bazaar. If cycling, escape the city ASAP — traffic is intense.

waypoints.name: Kashan
waypoints.notes: Fin Garden, historic houses. Desert edge. Easy day trip from Tehran by bus or perfect first cycling stop.

waypoints.name: Isfahan
waypoints.notes: Half the World. Naqsh-e Jahan Square, bridges, Armenian quarter. Allow 2-3 days minimum. Amir Kabir Hostel has secure bike/moto parking.

waypoints.name: Yazd
waypoints.notes: Wind catchers, Zoroastrian heritage, desert stargazing. The Isfahan→Yazd road crosses high desert — carry extra water if cycling.

waypoints.name: Shiraz
waypoints.notes: Persepolis, Pasargadae, gardens, poetry. End point. Fly out from Shiraz or continue south to Persian Gulf.

season_notes: Spring (Mar-May) and autumn (Sep-Nov) are ideal. Summer (Jul-Aug) is brutally hot in Yazd/Shiraz (40°C+). Winter is fine but cold in Isfahan/Tehran.
fuel_availability: Frequent on this route — every 50-80km. No remote stretches.
accommodation_notes: Hotels, hostels, and guesthouses in all cities. Wild camping possible between cities. Traditional caravanserais converted to hotels along the route.

name: Silk Road: Bazargan → Tabriz → Tehran → Mashhad → Turkmenistan
short_description: West-to-east Silk Road traverse entering from Turkey, exiting to Central Asia
fuel_availability: Good throughout. Longest gap ~100km in eastern Khorasan.

name: Caspian Coast: Astara → Rasht → Ramsar → Sari → Gorgan
short_description: Lush green coast ride through Iran's verdant north — rice paddies, forests, and Hyrcanian jungles
season_notes: Summer is humid and rainy. Spring and autumn are perfect. Winter is mild but grey.

name: Persian Gulf: Bandar Abbas → Qeshm → Hormuz
short_description: Island hopping in the Strait of Hormuz — rainbow mountains, mangroves, and fish markets
season_notes: Winter only! Summer temperatures exceed 45°C with extreme humidity.

name: Western Mountains: Kermanshah → Sanandaj → Hamadan → Zanjan
short_description: Kurdish highlands, Zagros passes, and ancient Median sites

name: Lut Desert Crossing: Kerman → Shahdad → Nebka → Kerman
short_description: Into the hottest place on Earth — Dasht-e Lut UNESCO site for the adventurous
season_notes: WINTER ONLY. Lut Desert holds the world record for highest surface temperature (70.7°C). Only November–February is safe.

name: Caucasus Circuit: Tabriz → Norduz → Armenia → Georgia → (back to Tabriz)
short_description: Cross into Armenia from northwestern Iran and loop through the Caucasus

name: Alborz Passes: Tehran → Chalus Road → Caspian → Haraz Road → Tehran
short_description: Iran's most dramatic mountain roads — switchbacks from 2000m to sea level
